title: Which Jim Carrey Character Are You, Deep Down?
dek: Eight iconic roles, eight wildly different ways to lose your mind on camera — answer honestly and find out which one is secretly you.

quiz.intro: No wrong answers here — just go with your gut and let the rubber face guide you.

questions.question: Your friend group is spiraling into chaos at a party. What's your move?

answers.text: I become the loudest, weirdest person in the room to fix it
I let out the version of myself I usually keep bottled up
I quietly realize something feels... staged, and I need to leave
I commit to a bit so hard nobody can tell if I'm joking anymore

questions.question: How do you actually handle a breakup?

answers.text: Feel two totally different emotions at once and let both win
Start questioning if the relationship was ever "real" at all
Turn the whole thing into a legendary, confusing performance
Beg the universe for a redo, specifically
A cozy night in that feels a little too perfect
An audience, any audience, watching my every move
Granted one wish, and using it selfishly at first
Leaving cryptic clues for people to find later

questions.question: How do you deal with being the center of attention?

answers.text: I crave it desperately, even when the crowd hates what I'm doing
I get drunk on it fast and start abusing the power
I want to be noticed for how clever I am, specifically
I don't need a crowd — just one person's undivided attention will do

questions.question: What's your relationship with the truth?

answers.text: I've been known to bend reality itself when it's convenient
I leave riddles instead of answers because directness is boring
I tell myself we're closer than we actually are
I'd genuinely rather erase a painful truth than sit with it

questions.question: Your boss gives you unlimited power for a day. What happens?

answers.text: I use it to out-think and outmaneuver everyone around me
I use it to get closer to people who didn't ask to be that close
I quietly use it to fix one small, personal regret
I use it to solve an absolutely ridiculous case

questions.question: How do you actually feel about being watched or being famous?

answers.text: I just want ONE person to really, truly see me
Honestly, I'd trade the spotlight for peace and quiet
Bring it on, the weirder the audience the better
I love the spotlight, as long as I get to control which "me" shows up

questions.question: What's your biggest flaw, if you're honest?

answers.text: I run from pain instead of sitting with it
I take a bit too far and can't read the room
I let my alter ego cause problems my "real self" has to clean up
I'm too trusting of the world around me until I'm not

questions.question: Pick a superpower you'd actually want.

answers.text: Talking to animals (and being a little unhinged about it)
Becoming a completely different, fearless version of myself on command
Finally seeing the world exactly as it really is
Making people believe absolutely anything about me

questions.question: Last one: how do you want people to remember you?

answers.text: As someone who finally let their true colors show
As someone who chose the hard, real world over a comfortable lie
As a legend nobody could ever quite figure out
As someone who learned real power means helping others

results.name: Ace Ventura
results.subtitle: Ace Ventura: Pet Detective (1994)
results.description: You're pure, unfiltered chaos energy and you wouldn't have it any other way. This is the role that turned Jim Carrey into a household name overnight, built entirely on rubbery physical comedy and a total refusal to read the room — and honestly, that's you at a party.

results.name: Stanley Ipkiss / The Mask
results.subtitle: The Mask (1994)
results.description: You've got a whole different personality just waiting for permission to come out. Carrey played the mild-mannered bank clerk and his manic green alter ego in the same film, the same year that also introduced the world to Kate Winslet — and you get exactly why Stanley needed that mask to finally let loose.

results.name: Truman Burbank
results.subtitle: The Truman Show (1998)
results.description: You've got a nagging feeling that something about your life isn't quite as real as it seems, and you're brave enough to chase that feeling anyway. This role was Carrey's big pivot into serious drama, earning him a Golden Globe even after the Academy famously snubbed him.

results.name: Andy Kaufman
results.subtitle: Man on the Moon (1999)
results.description: You commit to the bit so hard nobody can ever tell where the performance ends and you begin. Carrey's dedication to playing Kaufman was so extreme that he reportedly stayed in character both on and off set for the entire shoot, a process later documented in the film Jim & Andy: The Great Beyond.

results.name: Bruce Nolan
results.subtitle: Bruce Almighty (2003)
results.description: Give you an inch of power and you'll take a mile, at least until you learn the lesson everyone saw coming. Bruce spends most of the movie abusing God-given powers for petty personal gain before finally figuring out what actual responsibility looks like.

results.name: The Riddler
results.subtitle: Batman Forever (1995)
results.description: You need everyone to know you're the smartest person in the room, and you'll leave a trail of clues just to prove it. Edward Nygma's descent into gleeful, neon-green supervillainy remains one of the most over-the-top performances of Carrey's career, campy on purpose.

results.name: The Cable Guy
results.subtitle: The Cable Guy (1996)
results.description: You want closeness so badly you sometimes forget healthy boundaries exist. This darkly comic role was a deliberate swerve for Carrey into unsettling territory, and while it baffled audiences expecting straight comedy in 1996, it's since been reassessed as ahead of its time.

results.name: Joel Barish
results.subtitle: Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind (2004)
results.description: You're quiet, introspective, and you'd rather retreat inward than perform for anyone. Director Michel Gondry deliberately cast Carrey against type here, and even restricted him from improvising, to strip away the manic energy and expose something rawer and sadder underneath.
